skill.name: agent-tools
skill.description: Use the inference.sh CLI to discover and run hosted AI apps for image, video, language, search, audio, 3D, and Twitter/X workflows.
skill.summary: Run hosted inference.sh AI apps from Claude, Codex, or Claude Code.
skill.author: inference-sh-3

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, filesystem, and environment-variable patterns. Review shows most command hits are Markdown CLI examples or allowed `infsh` usage, but the skill does recommend `curl | sh`, stores credentials locally, and can submit user data to hosted third-party AI apps. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Network Installer Piped to Shell
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends running `curl -fsSL https://cli.inference.sh | sh`. This is a real supply-chain risk because a remote script executes immediately in the user environment, even though the file also provides a manual install path with checksum verification.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell command is directly present in multiple install instructions. The risk is mitigated by the documented manual install option, so this is not enough to prove malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Credential Handling Requires User Caution
medium_findings.description: The documentation instructs users to authenticate and optionally set `INFSH_API_KEY`. This is expected for an API CLI, but users could expose account credentials if they paste keys into shared prompts, logs, or shell history.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The API key references are explicit and legitimate for authentication. No evidence shows exfiltration, but credential exposure remains a meaningful operational risk.

medium_findings.title: Hosted App Execution Can Send User Data Externally
medium_findings.description: The core skill purpose is to run inference.sh hosted apps, including LLMs, search, media generation, and Twitter/X actions. This is legitimate functionality, but prompts and inputs may leave the local environment and reach third-party app providers.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files clearly describe hosted execution and URL-based outputs. This confirms external data flow, but it matches the stated purpose rather than hidden behavior.

low_findings.title: Command Injection Alerts Are Mostly Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Most static external-command detections are Markdown code blocks or command tables showing `infsh` usage. The skill metadata restricts tool access to `Bash(infsh *)`, and no arbitrary shell command construction was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The alerts point to documentation and fixed CLI examples, not dynamic code execution. The allowed-tools declaration further narrows execution to the intended CLI.

low_findings.title: SHA-256 Checksum Mention Is Not Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged several ordinary documentation lines as weak cryptography. The only relevant crypto context found is SHA-256 checksum verification for downloaded binaries, which is appropriate for integrity checking.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed static-hit locations do not show a weak cryptographic implementation. The only substantive cryptographic text describes SHA-256 checksum verification for installation.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Writes Are Installation and Completion Paths
low_findings.description: The hidden-path detections reference installing a binary under the user local bin path and writing shell completions under the Fish config directory. These are expected CLI setup locations, but users should review paths before running commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are visible setup commands, not hidden persistence code. They still touch user configuration paths, so the finding is low rather than safe.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Expected Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to inference.sh documentation, installer, distribution, output, and related docs. No evidence found of suspicious or unrelated exfiltration endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation or platform links matching the skill purpose. I did not find unrelated domains or concealed data transfer instructions.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe Remote Installer Into Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: The install instructions include direct execution of a remote installer with `curl | sh`, which can execute changed remote content before the user reviews it.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous shell pattern is directly present. It is documented as an install convenience, not hidden execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Substitution in Manual Install
dangerous_patterns.description: The manual install example derives a binary URL with nested shell commands. This is transparent documentation, but users should inspect resolved URLs before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command substitution is explicit and intended to select a binary. It depends on remote manifest content, which makes it riskier than a fixed download URL.

content.user_title: Run Cloud AI Apps with inference.sh
content.value_statement: AI agents often need one interface for many hosted models and media tools. This skill teaches Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to use the inference.sh CLI.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install and authenticate the inference.sh CLI.
Lists commands for discovering hosted apps by category, search term, and featured status.
Provides examples for running image, video, LLM, search, audio, 3D, and Twitter/X apps.
Explains task tracking, background runs, saved outputs, and result retrieval.
Documents app creation, local testing, deployment, and pulling app source through `infsh`.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI and a valid account or API key.
Runs hosted services, so prompts and inputs may leave the local environment.
Does not validate the safety, pricing, or data policy of each individual app.
Installation examples include shell commands that users should review before execution.

use_cases.title: Prototype AI media workflows
use_cases.description: Generate images, videos, audio, and 3D outputs from a single CLI workflow.

use_cases.title: Call hosted LLM and search tools
use_cases.description: Run OpenRouter models, Claude routes, Tavily, and Exa from repeatable terminal commands.

use_cases.title: Operate deployed inference.sh apps
use_cases.description: Discover apps, run tasks, check status, save outputs, and manage app deployment commands.

prompt_templates.title: Find an app
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the agent-tools skill to find inference.sh apps for image generation. Show the safest command to list options.

prompt_templates.title: Run a sample task
prompt_templates.prompt: Use inference.sh to generate a sample input for a video app, then explain how I should edit and run it.

prompt_templates.title: Track a long job
prompt_templates.prompt: I submitted an inference.sh task without waiting. Show how to check status and save the final result.

prompt_templates.title: Compare model routes
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan an inference.sh workflow that compares an LLM route, a search app, and an image app. Include privacy cautions.

output_examples.input: Find an app for fast video generation.
output_examples.output: The skill suggests searching inference.sh video apps, reviewing app details, generating sample input, and running the selected app with the inference.sh run command.

output_examples.input: Help me install the CLI safely.
output_examples.output: The skill explains the quick installer and the manual checksum-based path, with a reminder to review remote scripts before execution.

output_examples.input: Run a cloud image model and save the result.
output_examples.output: The skill guides app selection, input preparation, task execution, status checks, and saving the resulting media URL or output file.

best_practices: Review installer scripts and prefer the manual checksum path when possible.
Use environment variables or secret storage carefully, and never paste API keys into shared prompts.
Check app details and input schemas before sending private or regulated data to hosted apps.
anti_patterns: Do not pipe remote installers into a shell without reviewing the source and checksum path.
Do not send secrets, customer data, or unpublished assets to hosted apps without policy approval.
Do not assume every listed app has the same cost, license, retention, or moderation policy.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It teaches an AI assistant how to use the inference.sh CLI to discover and run hosted AI apps.

faq.question: Does it require an inference.sh account?
faq.answer: Yes. Authentication is required for most commands, either through login or an API key.

faq.question: Can it generate images and videos?
faq.answer: Yes. The documentation includes examples for FLUX, Veo, Seedance, and other hosted media apps.

faq.question: Is it safe to install automatically?
faq.answer: The quick install uses `curl | sh`, which has supply-chain risk. Review the script or use the manual checksum path.

faq.question: Can it access local files?
faq.answer: The documented CLI can read input files and save outputs when the user chooses those commands.

faq.question: Can it post to Twitter or X?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill lists Twitter/X automation apps, so users should confirm account permissions before running them.
